title: Pronouns: subject, object, and possessive
lesson: Subject pronouns (I, he, they) come before the verb; object pronouns (me, him, them) come after a verb or preposition. Possessive adjectives (my, her, their) sit before a noun, while possessive pronouns (mine, hers, theirs) stand alone with no noun after.

items.text: This is my brother. {1} is a doctor.
items.explanation: Use the subject pronoun before the verb: He is a doctor.

items.text: I like Anna. I see {1} every day. {2} dog is very friendly.
items.explanation: Object pronoun after a verb (I see her); possessive adjective before a noun (Her dog).
Use the possessive pronoun when no noun follows: This book is mine.

items.text: We love {1} new house. The neighbours said {2} was very nice.
items.explanation: Possessive adjective before a noun (our new house); subject pronoun for the house (it was very nice).
Use the object pronoun after a verb: help me.
Subject pronouns before verbs (I like; he helps); object pronoun after a verb (helps us).
Use the subject pronoun before the verb: They are my friends.

items.text: Is this your pen? Yes, it is {1}.
items.explanation: Use the possessive pronoun when no noun follows: it is yours.
Use the possessive adjective 'its' before a noun: its paws. (Note: 'it's' means 'it is'.)

items.text: That is John's bag. The blue one is {1}.
items.explanation: 'His' can stand alone as a possessive pronoun: the blue one is his.

items.text: I called {1} but {2} did not answer the phone.
items.explanation: Object pronoun after a verb (I called them); subject pronoun before a verb (they did not answer).
